content.full_text:
Law Offices of

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH

March 25, 1991

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Legal Division
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 "J" Street, Suite 600
Sacramento, California 95814

RE: REQUEST FOR FORMAL WRITTEN ADVICE

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

INTRODUCTION

I write to request formal written advice for
myself. I am a consultant to the Rules Committee of
the California State Senate.

FACTS

I am a designated employee of the California State
Senate, with "Article 2 type disclosure." Hence I am
subject to the new limits on gifts and honoraria.
Because I have full disclosure, any source of a gift,
regardless of geographic location, is a source as to
which the limits apply. (Gov. Code Sections 89503 and
89505.)

On or about February 5, 1991, Lance Olson,
Managing Partner of our law firm, received a telephone
inquiry regarding the availability of someone from our
firm to speak at the Annual Conference of the State
Governmental Affairs Council (SGAC) in Austin, Texas on
March 18-20, 1991. Mr. Olson, asked me if I was
willing to go on behalf of the firm. I agreed. SGAC
had offered to cover the airfare and lodging costs for
the member of the firm who attended.

The conference sponsor, SGAC, is a 501(c)(6)
organization based in Washington, D.C. Its members are
government relations officials for various companies.
They meet to discuss issues of common concern. This
year, because of developments in various states,
including California, SGAC wanted to hear about the new
ethics laws which recently took effect in California.
That was the topic for my talk.

The discussion on ethics consisted of a three-
person panel. I addressed the changes in the law; a
government affairs representative from IBM discussed
his company's internal ethics guidelines and practices.

HAND DELIVERED

[stamp marks visible on right side]

LANCE H. OLSON
BRUCE H. HAGEL
LEROY Y. FONG
ROBERT E. LEIDIGH
GEORGE M. WATERS
DIANE M. FISHBURN
CHRISTIAN A. SPECK

OF COUNSEL
LLOYD G. CONNELLY, Member
California State Legislature

300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, California 95814
TELEPHONE: (916) 442-2052
FAX: (916) 442-1280

Scott Hallabrin
March 25, 1991
Page 2

The panel moderator was a governmental affairs official with
American Express.

I was specifically invited to participate in the other
conference sessions. The conference began on Monday afternoon,
March 18. There was a dinner that evening (which I attended).

The next morning (March 19), there was a breakfast, then a
panel discussion, followed by a lunch. The afternoon was "free"
and then there was an evening barbecue meal. The last morning
(March 20) there was another group breakfast, then my panel on
ethics, followed by a wrap-up discussion session on issues of
concern. The conference adjourned at 12:30 p.m. on March 20.

I departed Sacramento on Monday morning March 18, arriving
at the conference site around 5:00 p.m. that evening. I promptly
met with the panel moderator and my co-panelist to discuss the
content of our panel discussion. This was the only mutually
convenient time for the three of us to meet before our panel.

I participated in most of the meals and other sessions. I
did not attend the barbecue dinner on March 19. Instead, I spent
that evening tending to personal business. I departed Austin on
the afternoon of March 20, returning that evening to Sacramento.

Prior to departing for Austin, I spent approximately 8 hours
preparing for my talk, including preparation of materials, an
outline, and research. During the conference, I spent
approximately 5 additional hours in preparation and delivering
the talk. In addition, I spent approximately 3 hours total
servicing clients of my firm via long-distance telephone.

While at the conference center, I purchased my own
beverages, but ate breakfast and lunch and one dinner with the
conferees. The conference was held at a conference center where
lodging for the conference was "full American plan." That meant
all meals were included in the room rate.

To date, my firm has picked up all costs with the exception
of the rental car, my beverages, meals in transit, and airport
parking. I paid for these myself. I have been asked by SGAC to
submit a travel reimbursement request for my airfare and lodging.
The lodging payment would include my meals at the conference. I
was there two nights (March 18 & 19).

While the topic of my speech (which lasted a little over one
hour) related to the subject matter area of the consulting work I
do for the Senate Rules Committee, my firm was contacted because
of its specialization in political law. The contact person and

Scott Hallabrin
March 25, 1991
Page 3

SGAC were initially seeking Mr. Olson's participation and were
completely unaware of my relationship with the Senate Rules
Committee.

QUESTIONS

1.)  Would it be a prohibited honorarium for the firm to be
reimbursed for airfare and both nights' lodging at the conference
site?

2.)  If so, which portion would not be permissible?

3.)  Should any permitted reimbursements to the firm be
reported by me on my annual statement of economic interests?  My
ownership interest in the firm is less than 10%.

4.)  If not prohibited and reportable, which portions, if
any, would be deemed a "gift" and which would be deemed
("earned") "income"?

5.)  If any portion is deemed a "gift" to me, is that
portion subject to the $250 annual limit?

In addressing these questions, please discuss the effect of
the fact that the reimbursements are to my firm, to which the
invitation was originally extended.  Are the payments to me
"earned income" on my part from the firm, because the personal
services which I provided are those which I customarily provide
to the firm as part of my work with the firm?

REQUEST

I need to prepare a timely request for reimbursement for the
firm for the expenses.  I would therefore ask that you expedite
your response to this request if at all possible.  I will await
your response before I submit the firm's reimbursement request.

Very truly yours,

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH

[signature]
ROBERT E. LEIDIGH

/ccz


3

sections.question:
1.) Would it be a prohibited honorarium for the firm to be reimbursed for airfare and both nights' lodging at the conference site?

2.) If so, which portion would not be permissible?

3.) Should any permitted reimbursements to the firm be reported by me on my annual statement of economic interests? My ownership interest in the firm is less than 10%.

4.) If not prohibited and reportable, which portions, if any, would be deemed a "gift" and which would be deemed ("earned") "income"?

5.) If any portion is deemed a "gift" to me, is that portion subject to the $250 annual limit?

In addressing these questions, please discuss the effect of the fact that the reimbursements are to my firm, to which the invitation was originally extended. Are the payments to me "earned income" on my part from the firm, because the personal services which I provided are those which I customarily provide to the firm as part of my work with the firm?

REQUEST

I need to prepare a timely request for reimbursement for the firm for the expenses. I would therefore ask that you expedite the response to this request if at all possible. I will await your response before I submit the firm's reimbursement request.

Scott Hallabrin  
March 25, 1991  

SGAC were initially seeking Mr. Olson's participation and were completely unaware of my relationship with the Senate Rules Committee.
